abstract:
This paper examines the prevalence and determinants of maternal morbidity in Kenya, a critical but often overlooked dimension of reproductive health in Sub-Saharan Africa. While maternal mortality receives significant scholarly and policy attention, morbidity remains under-researched due to the scarcity of clinical data and the absence of robust vital registration systems. Utilizing data from the 1998 Kenya Demographic and Health Survey (DHS)—the only national survey in Kenya to collect detailed information on delivery complications—this study analyzes women’s self-reports of four major obstetric complications: prolonged labor, excessive bleeding, high fever with foul-smelling discharge, and convulsions.

The findings reveal that more than half of the respondents reported at least one complication, with prolonged labor and hemorrhage identified as the most frequent morbidities. The analysis demonstrates that reports of maternal morbidity are significantly influenced by parity, ethnicity, and the type of assistance received during delivery, alongside notable regional variations. Beyond the empirical analysis, the paper critically evaluates the methodological strengths and limitations of using self-reported data versus clinical observations in developing contexts. It addresses potential biases, such as recall accuracy and selection bias (near-miss survivors), yet concludes that in settings with high home-delivery rates and weak infrastructure, population-based self-reports currently offer the most viable means for estimating the national burden of maternal illness and informing maternal healthcare priorities.
